Home / Tag Your Bag

Digitising Gift-aid registration across Oxfam's high-street stores.

  • Oxfam (via PXTech)

Redesigning a fragile service interaction that was costing Oxfam significant revenue each year.

Oxfam asked PXTech to explore whether redundant in-store Point-of-Sale hardware could be repurposed as self-service Gift Aid registration kiosks.

I led the product from proof of concept to live in-store usability validation, defining the service flow, UI and front-end implementation.

Role

  • Stakeholder liaison
  • Process and flow design
  • Interface and on-brand visual design
  • Front-end implementation
  • In-situ usability testing and validation

Problem

Oxfam estimated it was missing out on around £1m per year due to incomplete or illegible Gift Aid forms.

The existing process relied on handwritten forms that were later manually processed and transcribed. This created three core issues:

  • Lost revenue through incomplete or invalid registrations
  • High administrative overhead
  • Risk of non-compliance with legal requirements

At the same time, Oxfam had recently upgraded POS terminals across ~650 UK stores. The question was whether the old touchscreen units could be repurposed as self-service Gift Aid kiosks.

The hardware proof of concept was in place. What was missing was a structured experience that people could navigate confidently and consistently.

Approach

When I joined the project, the content and legal copy were sitting in spreadsheets. The proposed flow felt fragmented and overly complex.

Stakeholders initially leaned toward collecting additional information “just in case.” I made the case that every extra field could reduce completion rates and increase drop-off — directly undermining the revenue recovery goal.

While working closely with stakeholders at Oxfam’s head office, I reframed the approach around a simple principle: collect only what is essential.

Key decisions

  • Move eligibility and donation-type questions to the start
  • Reduce required fields to the legal minimum
  • Group contact details into clear, logical sections
  • Structure the flow semantically rather than mirroring the spreadsheet

Success also depended on buy-in from store volunteers, many of whom were non-technical and cautious about change.

I requested that one of the 20 trial stores be local to our office, allowing me to spend time in-store observing, testing and building trust with staff and volunteers.

An accessible, 'stakeholder friendly' flowchart helped me get buy-in for a simplified linear approach.

Build

With the flow agreed and legal requirements clarified, I designed firstly in Sketch, and then increasingly in the browser.

Constraints

  • Low-resolution touchscreen hardware
  • On-screen keyboard reducing usable content area to ~1024 × 500
  • Shared in-store WiFi with unstable connectivity

Design decisions

  • Larger form fields and scaled typography for legibility
  • Clear step emphasis to reinforce progress
  • Strong visual hierarchy optimised for touch interaction
  • Branded but simplified colour palette
  • Carefully chosen start and end imagery for reassurance and clarity

I created a webfont variation of the brand typeface and iterated layouts live in-browser to work within the tight screen constraints.

As testing revealed connectivity issues across stores, I introduced clearer error messaging and built greater resilience into the experience so staff could recover quickly when WiFi dropped.

Usability testing happened in store with real donors and shop staff.

Testing

Testing happened in context, with real donors and volunteers.

Initial volunteer reactions were cautious. Rather than defend the design, I focused on explaining the purpose of the project — recovering lost charity income — and demonstrating how the kiosk reduced manual admin.

That shift in framing helped adoption.

Findings

  • Volunteer confidence was fragile; unclear structure increased hesitation.
  • Users needed clearer signalling of progress and completion.
  • The Nectar integration point created uncertainty as to purpose.
  • Store WiFi instability posed a risk to completion and operational confidence.

Being physically present in-store allowed me to observe hesitation, workarounds and environmental constraints in real time.

Response

I refined the flow to prioritise eligibility upfront, reduced supporting copy to essential guidance, and strengthened visual step progression to reinforce completion.

I introduced clearer Nectar branding at the integration point and simplified language around data use to improve trust.

Working with engineering, I built in connectivity safeguards and clearer recovery messaging to mitigate network instability.

The revised design was validated through further live testing before full rollout.

Sample kiosk interface screens from the Tag Your Bag and cash donations flows.

Impact

The trial proved viable and demonstrated a clear uplift in data quality. Following full rollout across approximately 650 UK stores, invalid Gift Aid registrations reduced by around 50%, contributing to the retention of an estimated £500k in previously lost annual income.

The kiosks went on to become a white-label solution available to charities nationwide.

Kiosk spotted in an Oxfam store ~3 years after I completed the project. While the UI has evolved in line with branding, the principles I defined remain intact.

  • Ben Taylor — UX designer
